content.text: Once a trigger assessment confirms a DPIA is required, the DPIA itself must satisfy Article 35(7) of GDPR, which specifies four mandatory components: a systematic description of the processing, an assessment of necessity and proportionality, an assessment of the risks to individuals, and a description of the measures to address those risks. These components map directly to the three-part template structure used in this document.

A DPIA is not a one-time checkbox. It is a living document that must be revisited whenever the processing changes materially — new data categories, a new model provider, a new feature capability, or a significant increase in data volume all trigger a DPIA update.

The ICO's DPIA guidance makes clear that the document must be sufficiently detailed to allow an independent reviewer — including a supervisory authority — to understand the processing, the risks, and how those risks are controlled. Vague descriptions of 'appropriate safeguards' without specifics will not satisfy a regulatory examination.

attrs.alt: DPIA three-part structure: description, risk register, and mitigation plan for GenAI features

content.text: Part A — Systematic Description of Processing

State why the GenAI feature exists, what problem it solves, and the Article 6 lawful basis for processing personal data. For most enterprise GenAI features this will be legitimate interest (Article 6(1)(f)) or contract performance (Article 6(1)(b)). Where special category data is involved, an Article 9 condition must also be identified. The legitimate interest basis requires a three-part LIA (Legitimate Interest Assessment): the interest is legitimate, processing is necessary for it, and the interest overrides data subjects' rights.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional

class LawfulBasis(str):
    CONSENT = "Art.6(1)(a) consent"
    CONTRACT = "Art.6(1)(b) contract performance"
    LEGAL_OBLIGATION = "Art.6(1)(c) legal obligation"
    VITAL_INTERESTS = "Art.6(1)(d) vital interests"
    PUBLIC_TASK = "Art.6(1)(e) public task"
    LEGITIMATE_INTEREST = "Art.6(1)(f) legitimate interest"

class Art9Condition(str):
    EXPLICIT_CONSENT = "Art.9(2)(a) explicit consent"
    EMPLOYMENT_LAW = "Art.9(2)(b) employment law obligation"
    VITAL_INTERESTS = "Art.9(2)(c) vital interests"
    NOT_FOR_PROFIT = "Art.9(2)(d) not-for-profit body"
    MADE_PUBLIC = "Art.9(2)(e) manifestly made public"
    LEGAL_CLAIMS = "Art.9(2)(f) legal claims"
    SUBSTANTIAL_PUBLIC_INTEREST = "Art.9(2)(g) substantial public interest"
    MEDICAL = "Art.9(2)(h) medical purposes"
    PUBLIC_HEALTH = "Art.9(2)(i) public health"
    RESEARCH = "Art.9(2)(j) research/statistics"

@dataclass
class ProcessingDescription:
    feature_name: str
    version: str
    purpose: str
    lawful_basis: str
    art9_condition: Optional[str]   # required if special category data processed
    special_category_data_types: list[str]
    
    def requires_lia(self) -> bool:
        return self.lawful_basis == LawfulBasis.LEGITIMATE_INTEREST
    
    def is_legally_complete(self) -> bool:
        has_basis = bool(self.lawful_basis)
        has_art9 = not self.special_category_data_types or bool(self.art9_condition)
        return has_basis and has_art9
Describe every data flow in the feature's processing chain. For a GenAI feature this typically covers:
User inputs the prompt via the application UI.
Application adds system prompt context (which may contain user-specific data from the session or database).
The combined prompt is sent over HTTPS to the model provider's API endpoint.
The model generates a response, which is returned to the application.
The application may store the prompt/response pair in its own database for history, analytics, or fine-tuning purposes.
Usage metadata (user ID, timestamp, token count) is logged to the SIEM.
Each step must identify: what data moves, where it goes, whether encryption is applied in transit and at rest, and which party is the controller versus processor at each hop.

List who is affected and what data about them is processed. For GenAI features, data subjects commonly include the end user submitting prompts, any third parties mentioned in those prompts (customers, colleagues), and employees whose data appears in RAG source documents.
Name, email, session ID, prompt content
Name, job title, email (varies by use)
Name, role, performance data (if HR docs indexed)
Every third party that receives personal data must be identified. For GenAI features the minimum is the model provider. The DPA with each processor must confirm:
The provider acts only on documented instructions and does not use customer data for model training.
Data residency — where prompts are processed and stored.
Sub-processor disclosure — which companies the provider uses for infrastructure (GPU cloud, CDN, etc.).
Breach notification — the provider will notify within the time window required by the customer's regulatory obligations.
A DPIA risk register catalogs each risk to data subjects' rights and freedoms arising from the processing. For GenAI features, the standard risk categories are:

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class Likelihood(str, Enum):
    LOW = "low"        # unlikely given controls already in place
    MEDIUM = "medium"  # possible without specific mitigation
    HIGH = "high"      # likely or already observed

class Severity(str, Enum):
    LOW = "low"        # minor inconvenience; easily remedied
    MEDIUM = "medium"  # material harm; distress; financial loss
    HIGH = "high"      # serious harm; discrimination; identity theft; physical harm

RISK_SCORE: dict[tuple, int] = {
    (Likelihood.LOW, Severity.LOW): 1,
    (Likelihood.LOW, Severity.MEDIUM): 2,
    (Likelihood.LOW, Severity.HIGH): 3,
    (Likelihood.MEDIUM, Severity.LOW): 2,
    (Likelihood.MEDIUM, Severity.MEDIUM): 4,
    (Likelihood.MEDIUM, Severity.HIGH): 6,
    (Likelihood.HIGH, Severity.LOW): 3,
    (Likelihood.HIGH, Severity.MEDIUM): 6,
    (Likelihood.HIGH, Severity.HIGH): 9,
}

@dataclass
class DPIARisk:
    risk_id: str
    description: str
    affected_rights: list[str]       # e.g. ["privacy", "non-discrimination"]
    likelihood: Likelihood
    severity: Severity
    mitigation_measures: list[str]
    residual_likelihood: Optional[Likelihood] = None
    residual_severity: Optional[Severity] = None
    
    def inherent_score(self) -> int:
        return RISK_SCORE[(self.likelihood, self.severity)]
    
    def residual_score(self) -> Optional[int]:
        if self.residual_likelihood and self.residual_severity:
            return RISK_SCORE[(self.residual_likelihood, self.residual_severity)]
        return None
    
    def is_acceptable(self) -> Optional[bool]:
        score = self.residual_score()
        if score is None:
            return None
        return score <= 2  # Low residual risk
    
    def requires_sa_consultation(self) -> bool:
        score = self.residual_score()
        return score is not None and score >= 6
The following risks appear in almost every GenAI DPIA and should be assessed as a baseline:
Model hallucination generating false personal data
Training data inference revealing memorised PII
Retention of prompts beyond stated purpose
Third-party model training on customer prompts (if DPA absent)
Discriminatory output from biased training data
Subject access request cannot be satisfied for AI outputs
Data residency violation (prompts processed in wrong jurisdiction)

For each identified risk, the mitigation plan records the specific technical and organisational measures that reduce the risk to an acceptable residual level. Mitigations must be specific — 'we will use appropriate security measures' does not satisfy a DPIA; 'we will enforce TLS 1.3 in transit, AES-256 at rest, and retain logs for a maximum of 90 days with automated deletion' does.
Prompt governance layer blocking credential patterns
Output filter; rate limiting; RBAC on sensitive context
Human review gate before customer delivery
No-train clause in DPA; API-only (no data shared with provider for training)
Vendor audit; model version change if memorisation confirmed
90-day automatic log deletion; TTL configured at platform level
Pre-deployment bias testing; output review for protected characteristics
User feedback mechanism; quarterly output sampling
Log association of user ID to session; deletion on SAR request
Privacy notice update explaining AI output handling
After the risk register and mitigation plan are complete, the DPO reviews the assessment and records one of three outcomes:
Approved to proceed — all residual risks are Low or Acceptable; no supervisory authority consultation required.
Approved with conditions — specific mitigations must be implemented before go-live; the DPO sets a review date.
High residual risk remains — supervisory authority consultation required under Article 36 before the feature can launch.
The DPIA must be integrated into the feature release process so that it functions as an actual gate rather than a retrospective document. Concretely, this means:
The feature's launch checklist includes a field for the DPIA ID and DPO sign-off date. Features with a mandatory DPIA cannot receive launch approval without these.
The DPIA trigger assessment output (from the previous sub-subtopic) links to the DPIA record, creating a traceable chain from initial assessment to approved launch.
For features built in sprints, the DPIA completion sprint should be scheduled at least two sprints before the planned launch sprint to allow time for remediation if conditions are imposed.
The release pipeline can include an automated check that queries the DPIA registry — if no approved DPIA exists for the feature ID, the deployment is blocked.

A DPIA template for GenAI features structures the assessment across three parts: a systematic description of the processing (data flows, legal basis, processors, retention), a risk register scoring each identified risk by likelihood and severity, and a mitigation plan recording the specific controls that reduce each risk to an acceptable residual level before DPO sign-off.
Part A must describe data flows at each processing step — from user prompt through model API to output storage — identifying controller/processor roles at each hop.
The legal basis for processing must be identified explicitly; special category data requires both an Art.6 and Art.9 condition.
Part B risk register uses inherent likelihood × severity scoring; GenAI baseline risks include prompt injection, hallucinated PII, training data inference, and retention beyond purpose.
Part C mitigations must be specific and verifiable — 'appropriate measures' is insufficient; named technical controls with owners and dates are required.
If any residual risk remains High after mitigation, Article 36 supervisory authority consultation is mandatory before launch.
Embed the DPIA as an automated release gate: no approved DPIA ID + DPO sign-off = no deployment.
